title: The Macro Minute: What matters most to markets—Retail Sales, Kevin Warsh’s testimony, or ceasefire?

Happy Tuesday out there team 42. It's
your skipper here at Darius Dale
presenting our macro minute for Tuesday,
April 21st, 2026. As always, we'll start
with the executive summary from today's
lead up morning note. So, let's dive
right in. Today's key macro question is,
what matters most to asset markets?
March retail sales, Kevin Warsh's
testimony, or the US-Iran ceasefire? Our
answer is neither.
Each is a feature of the evolving
distribution of probable economic policy
and market outcomes. It's not our job to
tell the market what it must price in
and when. Rather, it's our job to notice
when the market regime changes faster
than investor consensus, so that we can
execute our buy and sell orders ahead of
the herd.
Regarding the data, March retail sales
supported our resilient US economy and
paradigm C themes. Paradigm C being run
it hot, that's the growth phase of the
cut grow print sequence required to
address the geopolitically driven supply
demand imbalance in the Treasury bond
market, which has remained our core
research view since the summer of 2023.
Getting back to the executive summary
here, Kevin Warsh's testimony is likely
to quell fears regarding his hawkish
views on the balance sheet. And finally,
the tenuous US-Iran ceasefire appears
headed for another round of
brinksmanship. We continue to anticipate
a positive resolution to the closure of
the Strait of Hormuz.
Transitioning to our 42 macro dashboard,
as always, wrap up question from our
community.
This one's titled liquidity, how fragile
is the recent signal pivot? It says, hi
team 42, just feeling good about the
recent switch to back to risk on. I came
across the Wall Street Journal reporting
about UAE approaching Treasury Secretary
Scott Bessent, former client, for access
to the US dollar in case the crisis
drags on. Is this a trailing news item
relating to former risk on regime risk
off regime, or hint of things to come? I
E data points getting worse soon? Might
the global liquidity signal switch again
to risk off? How volatile is this
signal? And so, excellent question here.
So, the first answer I'll say is that
the the UAE
the minister who reached out to Treasury
Secretary Scott Bessent, this is a
prospective market risk. What they're
essentially signaling is that, hey, if
the strait remains closed and we're
unable to sell our petroleum products to
generate the you know, how the profits
and household savings that we need to
reinvest back into your bond market, Mr.
Bessent, then you're going to have to
give us dollar swap lines in order to
accomplish that. Otherwise, we're going
to have to sell
you know, we're going to have to find
alternate means of
generating wealth. So, that's our step
one. So, the key takeaway being is that
if the strait remains closed, I E the
ceasefire and negotiations fail, then
we're talking about moving back into the
global liquidity crisis that we were
warning about back in early March when
those barrels of petroleum products and
those fertilizer inputs were being
embargoed, thus you know, throwing a
massive wrench in the dollar recycling
cycle, the global dollar recycling
cycle, you know, from the international
investment surplus economies in the Gulf
Coast and in Asia, you know, basically
you know, squashing their ability to
generate profits, corporate profits,
household savings, and FX reserves that
then get recycled to all capital markets
in the world. That is the ultimate risk
in this conflict. It's not about, you
know, GDP going down or earnings going
down. It's the actual evaporation of
liquidity. So, that remains a
prospective market risk to the extent
that we see the ceasefire negotiations
fail, but ultimately we think they will
not fail.
And so, the final thing I'll say is, you
know, in terms of being worried about
the market risk, you know, the first
thing I'll say is that, you know, you
don't have to sell the exact top or buy
the exact bottom of every market as our
Dr. Mo model here for the S&P indicates.
As long as you sell near the top and buy
near the bottom, you can maximize upside
capture in any trending bull market and
minimize downside capture in any
trending bear market. That's what that's
what these these these color coding
indicates. That the dark green is
max long max position, the red is a
short max position, the gray is a no
position, and the light green is a short
half position. So, the key takeaway I'll
say is that you don't have to sell the
top and buy and buy the exact bottom of
any market cycle. There's not been an
investor in history.
There's no not one investor in history
has proven the ability to do that. The
only people out there that are trying to
that are even attempting to do that are
gullible retail investors that have been
that have had their amygdalae tickled
by, you know, snake oil salesmen. The
reality is if you stop optimizing your
investment process for accomplishing
something that no investor in the
history of mankind has ever proven the
ability to do, and just optimize for
maximizing upside capture in trending
bull markets and minimize
and maximize and optimize for minimizing
downside capture in trending bear
markets, you're going to have
significantly better results than you
used to having and significantly better
results than the herd. So, we'll wrap it
up there. Darius Dale here presenting
our macro minute for Tuesday, April
21st, 2026. Best of luck out there
today. We'll catch back here tomorrow.
Cheers.
